course.title: Foundations of Math
course.summary: Build number sense, understand fractions, and connect decimals with percents through guided lessons and practice.

units.title: Number Sense
units.summary: Understand whole numbers, place value, and how to compare quantities.

units.title: Fractions
units.summary: Represent parts of a whole, find equivalent fractions, and add fractions with like denominators.

units.title: Decimals and Percent
units.summary: Connect fractions to decimals and percents for everyday comparisons.

units.title: OpenStax STEM (samples)
units.summary: Real excerpts from OpenStax Precalculus 2e and Introductory Statistics (CC-licensed).

lessons.title: Whole Numbers
lessons.body_md:
# Whole Numbers

Whole numbers are the counting numbers together with zero: 0, 1, 2, 3, and so on. We use them whenever we count objects, measure distance in whole steps, or label items in order.

## Why whole numbers matter

If you have three apples, the number **3** tells you how many apples there are. If a shelf holds zero books, **0** is still a meaningful quantity—it means none.

## Try it

Start at 12 and count forward by ones until you reach 20. Then count backward from 20 to 12. Say each number aloud and notice how each step adds or removes one item from your count.

## Check your understanding

Name one situation at home and one at school where you use whole numbers. Explain what each number counts.

objectives.statement: Define a whole number and give an example from daily life.
Count forward and backward by ones within 100.

lessons.title: Place Value
lessons.body_md:
# Place Value

In the number **347**, each digit has a different value because of its **place**. The 3 is in the hundreds place (300), the 4 is in the tens place (40), and the 7 is in the ones place (7).

## Expanded form

We can write 347 as:

**300 + 40 + 7**

This is called **expanded form**. It shows how place value builds the number.

## Try it

Write 528 in expanded form. Which digit is in the tens place? What value does it represent?

## Real-world connection

When you see a price tag of $215, the 2 stands for two hundreds of dollars, the 1 for one ten, and the 5 for five ones.

objectives.statement: Identify the value of each digit in a three-digit number.
Decompose a number into hundreds, tens, and ones.

lessons.title: Comparing Numbers
lessons.body_md:
# Comparing Numbers

We compare numbers to decide which is **greater**, which is **less**, or whether they are **equal**.

| Symbol | Meaning        |
| ------ | -------------- |
| >      | greater than   |
| <      | less than      |
| =      | equal to       |

## Strategy

Compare from left to right using place value. For 452 and 439, the hundreds digits are equal (4 = 4), but 5 tens is greater than 3 tens, so **452 > 439**.

## Try it

Compare 318 and 381. Write the correct symbol between them and explain your reasoning in one sentence.

objectives.statement: Use >, <, and = to compare two whole numbers.
Explain why one number is greater using place value.

lessons.title: Introduction to Fractions
lessons.body_md:
# Introduction to Fractions

A **fraction** represents parts of a whole. The top number (**numerator**) counts the parts you have; the bottom number (**denominator**) counts equal parts in the whole.

## Why fractions matter

Fractions appear everywhere: recipes, music, sports stats, and sharing pizza fairly with friends.

## Try it

If you cut a pizza into 4 equal slices and eat 1 slice, you ate **1/4** of the pizza. The numerator is 1 and the denominator is 4.

## Check your understanding

Draw or describe a situation where you would use the fraction **3/8**. Label the numerator and denominator.

objectives.statement: Explain what a fraction represents using a real-world example.
Identify the numerator and denominator in a given fraction.

lessons.title: Equivalent Fractions
lessons.body_md:
# Equivalent Fractions

**Equivalent fractions** name the same amount even though they look different. For example, **1/2**, **2/4**, and **3/6** all represent half of a whole.

## Multiply top and bottom by the same number

If you multiply the numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number, the value stays the same:

**1/2 = (1×2)/(2×2) = 2/4**

## Try it

Find two fractions equivalent to **3/5**. Show your work.

## Reasoning check

Why does multiplying both parts by 2 not change the amount? Explain using a picture or words.

objectives.statement: Define equivalent fractions in your own words.
Create an equivalent fraction by multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number.

lessons.title: Adding Fractions with Like Denominators
lessons.body_md:
# Adding Fractions with Like Denominators

When fractions have the **same denominator**, you add the numerators and keep the denominator:

**2/7 + 3/7 = 5/7**

Think of seventh-sized slices: 2 slices plus 3 slices make 5 slices, still each one-seventh of the whole.

## Why denominators must match

Denominators tell you the **size** of each part. You can only combine parts of the same size directly.

## Try it

Compute **1/8 + 4/8**. Then write a word problem that this equation could represent.

objectives.statement: Add fractions that share the same denominator.
Explain why denominators must match before adding.

lessons.title: Introduction to Decimals
lessons.body_md:
# Introduction to Decimals

A **decimal** uses a dot (decimal point) to show parts of a whole. In **0.75**, the 7 is in the tenths place (0.7) and the 5 is in the hundredths place (0.05).

## Fraction connection

**0.5** means five tenths, which is the same as **5/10** or **1/2**.

## Try it

Write 0.3 as a fraction in simplest form. Then compare 0.45 and 0.5 using < or >.

objectives.statement: Identify tenths and hundredths in a decimal number.
Compare two decimals using place value.

lessons.title: Fraction–Decimal Links
lessons.body_md:
# Fraction–Decimal Links

Every fraction can be viewed as division: **3/4** means 3 ÷ 4, which equals **0.75**.

## Common conversions

| Fraction | Decimal |
| -------- | ------- |
| 1/2      | 0.5     |
| 1/4      | 0.25    |
| 3/4      | 0.75    |

## Try it

Convert **1/5** to a decimal. Show the division you used.

objectives.statement: Convert common fractions to decimals.
Explain how division connects fractions and decimals.

lessons.title: Introduction to Percents
lessons.body_md:
# Introduction to Percents

A **percent** is a ratio out of 100. **50%** means 50 out of 100, or half of a whole.

## Decimal link

Move the decimal point two places to convert:

- **0.25 → 25%**
- **75% → 0.75**

## Try it

Express **1/2**, **0.2**, and **40%** in all three forms (fraction, decimal, percent) where possible.

objectives.statement: Define percent as a ratio out of 100.
Convert between percents and decimals for benchmark values.

lessons.title: Introduction to Functions
lessons.body_md:
# Introduction to Functions

Toward the end of the twentieth century, the values of stocks of internet and technology companies rose dramatically. As a result, the Standard and Poor's stock market average rose as well. The graph tracks the value of an initial investment of just under $100 over 40 years — it shows that an investment worth less than $500 until about 1995 skyrocketed to about $1,100 by the beginning of 2000.

That five-year period became known as the **dot-com bubble** because so many internet startups were formed. As bubbles tend to do, the dot-com bubble eventually burst. Many companies grew too fast and then suddenly went out of business, causing the sharp decline beginning at the end of 2000.

Notice that there is a definite relationship between the year and stock market average. For any year we choose, we can determine the corresponding value of the stock market average. In this chapter, we will explore these kinds of relationships and their properties.

## Chapter outline

- 1.1 Functions and Function Notation
- 1.2 Domain and Range
- 1.3 Rates of Change and Behavior of Graphs
- 1.4 Composition of Functions
- 1.5 Transformation of Functions
- 1.6 Absolute Value Functions
- 1.7 Inverse Functions

---

*Source: [OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch. 1](https://openstax.org/books/precalculus-2e/pages/1-introduction-to-functions) · License: CC BY 4.0*

objectives.statement: Describe how input and output quantities relate in real-world models such as stock trends.
Identify the sections covered in the functions chapter of Precalculus 2e.

lessons.title: 1.1 Functions and Function Notation
lessons.body_md:
# 1.1 Functions and Function Notation

In this section, you will:

- Determine whether a relation represents a function.
- Find the value of a function.
- Determine whether a function is one-to-one.
- Use the vertical line test to identify functions.
- Graph the functions listed in the library of functions.

A jetliner changes altitude as its distance from the starting point of a flight increases. The weight of a growing child increases with time. In each case, one quantity depends on another — there is a relationship between the two quantities that we can describe, analyze, and use to make predictions.

## Relations and functions

A **relation** is a set of ordered pairs. The set of first components is the **domain**; the set of second components is the **range**.

A **function** \(f\) is a relation that assigns a single value in the range to each value in the domain — no \(x\)-values are repeated.

Given a relationship between two quantities, determine whether the relationship is a function:

1. Identify the input values.
2. Identify the output values.
3. If each input leads to only one output, classify the relationship as a function.

## Function notation

We use \(f(x)\) to name the output value of a function when the input is \(x\). For example, if \(f(x) = x^2 - 5\), then \(f(3) = 4\).

---

*Source: [OpenStax Precalculus 2e §1.1](https://openstax.org/books/precalculus-2e/pages/1-1-functions-and-function-notation) · License: CC BY 4.0*

objectives.statement: Determine whether a relation represents a function.
Evaluate a function using function notation.

lessons.title: 1.2 Domain and Range
lessons.body_md:
# 1.2 Domain and Range

In this section, you will:

- Find the domain of a function defined by an equation.
- Find the range of a function.
- Graph piecewise-defined functions.

When we identify the **domain** of a function, we identify the set of all possible input values. When we identify the **range**, we identify the set of all possible output values.

## Domain from an equation

Many functions have domains that are all real numbers except values that make the denominator zero or produce a negative value under an even root.

## Interval notation

We often write domains and ranges using interval notation. For example, all real numbers greater than or equal to 3 can be written as \([3, \infty)\).

## Piecewise functions

A **piecewise-defined function** uses different rules over different parts of its domain. The absolute value function \(|x|\) is a common example.

---

*Source: [OpenStax Precalculus 2e §1.2](https://openstax.org/books/precalculus-2e/pages/1-2-domain-and-range) · License: CC BY 4.0*

objectives.statement: Find the domain of a function defined by an equation.
Express domain and range using interval notation.

lessons.title: 1.1 Definitions of Statistics, Probability, and Key Terms
lessons.body_md:
# 1.1 Definitions of Statistics, Probability, and Key Terms

The **science of statistics** deals with the collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data. We see and use data in our everyday lives — in news, sports, economics, and health.

## Descriptive vs inferential statistics

- **Descriptive statistics** organizes and summarizes data (graphs, averages).
- **Inferential statistics** uses formal methods and probability to draw conclusions from sample data about a population.

## Probability

**Probability** is a mathematical tool used to study randomness — the likelihood that an event occurs. With many repetitions, outcomes show a regular pattern even when individual outcomes are uncertain.

## Key terms

| Term | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Population | The entire collection under study |
| Sample | A subset of the population |
| Parameter | A numerical characteristic of the population |
| Statistic | A number computed from sample data that estimates a parameter |
| Variable | A characteristic that can be measured for each member (numerical or categorical) |
| Data | The actual values of the variable |

---

*Source: [OpenStax Introductory Statistics §1.1](https://openstax.org/books/introductory-statistics/pages/1-1-definitions-of-statistics-probability-and-key-terms) · License: CC BY 4.0*

objectives.statement: Differentiate between population, sample, parameter, and statistic.
Explain the role of probability in inferential statistics.
